query: effectiveness of ozanezumab in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Edaravone is a free radical scavenger that is thought to reduce oxidative stress and slow functional progression. Edaravone provides the most benefit in patients with early ALS (ie, less than two years of symptoms, living independently, VC >80%, and scores of 2 or more in all items of ALSFRS-R) with probable or definite ALS (revised El Escorial criteria). One study showed approximately 33% slower functional decline in patients at 24 weeks follow-up. [37] Edaravone treatment is expensive ($146,000 annually). Edaravone should be used with caution in patients with asthma. It contains sodium bisulfite, which may cause asthmatic reactions in up to 5% of patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Future therapeutic options
retrieved_docs.content: The use of antisense oligonucleotides in SOD 1 related and C9orf related ALS are subjects of research in future clinical trials. [93]

retrieved_docs.title: Locked-in Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatments for demyelinating diseases are variable. The treatment of LIS from central pontine myelinolysis is focused on supportive measures: gradual correction of sodium abnormalities and close monitoring of respiratory status is imperative. [31] Inflammatory neuropathies may be treated in a somewhat similar fashion. Guillain-Barre syndrome may be treated with IVIG, plasmapheresis, and monitored airway ventilation. [32] Exacerbations of multiple sclerosis may be treated with high-dose glucocorticoids acutely with relapses treated with disease-modifying agents and humanized monoclonal antibodies. [33] ALS may be treated with supportive measures in conjunction with long-term agents riluzole or edaravone to prolong survival. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: There is no cure for ALS. [5] [7] The median survival is 3 to 5 years. Approximately 30% of patients survive 5 years and 10% ten years. Riluzole provides a survival benefit averaging 3 months, with edaravone showing a 33% reduction in the decline of the ALS Functional Rating Scale–Revised (ALSFRS-R) at 6 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofersen -- Indications -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tofersen, a novel antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) drug, received accelerated approval from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in April 2023 for the treatment of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). ALS is a progressive, fatal neurodegenerative disorder affecting motor neurons in the brain and spinal cord, leading to muscle weakness, paralysis, and eventual death. Tofersen is specifically indicated for ALS patients with a superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1) gene mutation. The mutant SOD1 protein resulting from this gene mutation gains a toxic function and significantly increases the amount of SOD1 messenger RNA (mRNA) and protein in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease-modifying Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Edaravone has also been shown to impede disease progression in those patients with early-onset disease and rapid disease progression. [62] Its role in the management of all patients with ALS remains to be addressed. [63] The drug acts as an antioxidant, which has been shown to prevent the nitration of tyrosine residues in experimental animal models. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Tofersen -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Tofersen, a novel antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) drug, received accelerated approval from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in April 2023 for the treatment of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). ALS is a progressive, fatal neurodegenerative disorder affecting motor neurons in the brain and spinal cord, leading to muscle weakness, paralysis, and eventual death. Tofersen is specifically indicated for ALS patients with a superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1) gene mutation. The mutant SOD1 protein resulting from this gene mutation gains a toxic function and significantly increases SOD1 messenger RNA (mRNA) levels and protein in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). This activity provides comprehensive information about Tofersen's indications, contraindications, mechanism of action, adverse event profile, monitoring, and relevant interactions relevant to patients and healthcare team members involved in treating patients with ALS and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Riluzole, Edaravone, and sodium phenylbutyrate are the only approved medications to treat ALS but provide limited survival benefits. [1] [3] Riluzole, 50mg bid, is recommended for all patients with ALS and is thought to reduce glutamate-induced excitotoxicity. Riluzole is the only medication that improves overall survival (74% vs. 58% in the placebo group at 12 months) and slows the progression of symptoms (ie, survival without tracheostomy (57% vs. 50% at 18 months). Riluzole is most effective in patients who have been symptomatic for less than 5 years, have a vital capacity (VC) >60% of the predicted value, and do not require a tracheostomy. [36] It is important to note that the elimination of riluzole will be affected by CYP1A2 inhibitors like caffeine and theophylline (decrease the rate of elimination of riluzole). Transaminase elevation occurs in approximately 50% of patients. Monitor transaminases monthly for the first three months and every three months after.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofersen -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Screen potential ALS patients with SOD1 gene mutations and disease severity who may benefit from tofersen treatment. Apply best practices for pharmacovigilance to identify and manage any adverse events associated with tofersen use in patients to avoid treatment-related complications. Assess the response to tofersen treatment by regularly evaluating ALS symptoms, disease progression, and changes in quality of life following the therapy. Communicate effectively with patients and their caregivers about the potential benefits, risks, and expectations of tofersen therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: One classic presentation of ALS may not exist. Personalized therapy needs to be implemented. [3] Each patient must be evaluated for ALS mimics. Treatment at multidisciplinary ALS clinics improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the etiology of ALS. Identify the typical findings on the examination of a patient with ALS. Determine the interventions available for patients with ALS. Apply effective strategies to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to facilitate positive outcomes for patients with ALS. retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: As the disease progresses, there will be increased dysarthria and the inability to communicate. Speech therapy provides limited benefit, and appropriate alternative communication methods (writing, alphabet boards, and electronic assistive communication devices) are needed. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Tofersen -- Indications -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tofersen is not a cure for ALS, as its benefits are limited to a small subset of patients with SOD1 mutations. Patients with ALS without SOD1 mutation will not benefit from tofersen and, therefore, should not consider it a viable treatment option. Whether tofersen will improve ALS patients' survival or quality of life is uncertain. However, research has indicated that it could potentially slow down the progression of the disease. The clinical benefit of tofersen is currently being evaluated under the FDA's accelerated approval process and will be continuously monitored.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofersen -- Indications -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Mutations in the SOD1 gene represent the second-most common cause of familial ALS, occurring in approximately 10% to 20% of familial cases and approximately 1% to 2% of sporadic ALS cases. [2] [3] Unfortunately, ALS has no cure, and the existing treatments can only provide modest symptomatic relief.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating a potential therapeutic role for this vitamin in managing the neurological disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1298
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolen and vitamin B12 are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for multiple sclerosis, suggesting that each may be used to help manage this demyelinating neurological disorder. Their inclusion highlights the exploration of diverse treatment options in the care of patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1659
retrieved_docs.content: Nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, produces a physiologic effect described as increased protein synthesis; this enhancement of protein production, particularly of the survival motor neuron (SMN) protein, underlies its therapeutic benefit in disorders such as spinal muscular atrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serves as a parent category that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. Nedokromil is identified as a member of the class of antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids, indicating its use in respiratory conditions where allergy‑mediated bronchoconstriction is a concern. Nusinersen, while also belonging to the broader N [Preparations] group, is a distinct therapeutic agent used for the treatment of spinal muscular atrophy, reflecting a neuromuscular application rather than a respiratory one. This organization demonstrates that the N [Preparations] category encompasses a range of medications with different mechanisms and clinical indications, from inhaled antiallergic agents like nedokromil to antisense oligonucleotide therapies such as nusinersen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, a corticosteroid with anti‑inflammatory properties, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي), highlighting its role in managing inflammatory and autoimmune neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1364
retrieved_docs.content: Baclofen and dantrolene are both pharmacologic agents that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. These medications are considered for managing the muscle spasticity and related motor symptoms that commonly occur in patients with multiple sclerosis, offering therapeutic options to improve functional ability and quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1490
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolone, a corticosteroid medication, may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, offering anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that can help reduce disease activity. In addition, vitamin B12 is also considered a potential therapeutic agent for multiple sclerosis, as supplementation may support neurological function and address deficiencies that can exacerbate symptoms. Both methylprednisolone and vitamin B12 represent possible treatment options in the management of multiple sclerosis, highlighting the importance of a multifaceted therapeutic approach.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin and the corticosteroid formulations methylprednisolone aceponate and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, highlighting the potential role of corticosteroid‑based therapies in managing this demyelinating disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin, methylprednisolon acetate, and methylprednisolone aceponate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating that several corticosteroid‑related medications are considered potential therapeutic options for managing this demyelinating disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1912
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] is a categorical grouping of pharmaceutical agents that encompasses both nedokromil and nusinersen. Nedokromil is a mast‑cell stabilizer employed in the management of allergic airway diseases such as asthma, while nusinersen is an antisense oligonucleotide therapy indicated for spinal muscular atrophy. By classifying these distinct drugs under the umbrella of N [Preparations], the relationship highlights that they share a common designation as specialized medical preparations despite their differing mechanisms of action and therapeutic targets. This categorization aids clinicians and pharmacists in organizing and referencing these agents within broader drug formularies and treatment protocols.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, identified as a corticosteroid formulated for local oral treatment, is classified within the group of corticosteroids used for local oral administration. This medication may be employed in the therapeutic management of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). The association of triamcinolone with the class “Corticosteroids for local oral treatment” underscores its role as a locally administered anti‑inflammatory agent, while its potential use in multiple sclerosis highlights the broader application of corticosteroids in treating inflammatory demyelinating disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2308
retrieved_docs.content: Other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are linked to the presence of diffuse encephalitis around the axis, as indicated by الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, suggesting that these conditions may involve or utilize this widespread inflammatory process in the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1692
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolon, a glucocorticoid anti‑inflammatory medication, may be used in the treatment of both التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis) and تَشَنُّج قَصَبِيّ (spinal stenosis). By reducing inflammation and modulating immune responses, triamcinolon can help alleviate the pathological processes underlying these neurological disorders, offering a therapeutic option for patients affected by either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1823
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin and several corticosteroid formulations—including methylprednisolon acetate, methylprednisolone aceponate, and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate—are identified as potential therapeutic agents for multiple sclerosis. These drugs act by modulating immune activity and reducing inflammatory processes that contribute to demyelination and neurological deficits in multiple sclerosis. Their use, particularly during acute relapses, may help alleviate symptoms, limit lesion formation, and improve short‑term neurological function, highlighting the role of corticosteroid‑based therapies in the management of this chronic autoimmune disease.
